mappings.rationaleAI: Propositional and predicate logic, validity, axioms, and mathematical proofs span the modern development of logic (A.3, A.4). Coverage of modal logic and necessity/possibility relates to alternative logics (B.2.c). The treatment of logic's scope and symbolic notation addresses the nature and varieties of logic (B.1.a), and Godel's incompleteness theorems and computability connect to limitations of logic (B.1.b).

Aristotle's foundational contributions to formal logic, syllogistic, and modal logic directly address the logic of predicates (A.1.b). His relationship to Theophrastus and subsequent developments in predicate logic connect to later developments (A.1.c).
Plato's contributions to theories of language, the axiomatic method, and dialectical reasoning represent precursors of ancient logic (A.1.a) alongside the Sophists and Socrates.
The Socratic method of dialectical questioning represents a key precursor of ancient logic (A.1.a), contributing to theories of language and argumentation before Aristotle's formalization.

Presocratic exploration of reason, perception, and proto-scientific inquiry through figures like Parmenides and Heraclitus represents precursors of ancient logic (A.1.a) in their contributions to early theories of language and rational argument.

Stoic logic, particularly the contributions of Chrysippus and the school's development of propositional logic, directly addresses the founding of the logic of propositions through Stoic contributions (A.1.d).

Broad coverage of ancient Graeco-Roman philosophical traditions including Platonism, Aristotelianism, Stoicism, and Scepticism encompasses the entire period of ancient logic (A.1), including debates in logic across multiple schools.

Coverage of Boethius, Abelard, William of Ockham, and the problem of universals directly addresses medieval logic development (A.2). The distinctive literary forms and institutions of medieval philosophy relate to the transmission of logical traditions (A.2.a), and medieval theories of language connect to categorematic and syncategorematic terms (A.2.b).
Arabic contributions to philosophy including Aristotelian influences and engagement with reason connect to the development of medieval logic through Arabic contributions (A.2.a).
Avicenna's innovations in logic and his influence on Latin scholastic philosophy directly address Arabic contributions to the development of medieval logic (A.2.a).

Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine, including engagement with Islamic philosophy and scholastic method, contributes to medieval developments in formal logic (A.2.c) and the broader medieval logical tradition (A.2).
Descartes's methodological doubt and mathematical approach to natural science connect to logic in the Renaissance (A.3.a) and the influence of the rise of the natural sciences on logical thought.
Leibniz's contributions to logic including the characteristica universalis and general calculus of reasoning directly address the rise of mathematical logic during the Enlightenment (A.3.b).

Kant's synthesis of rationalism and empiricism, his contributions to logic, and the categories of understanding relate to Enlightenment developments in logic (A.3.b) and to the organization of logic as a discipline (B.1).

Russell's Principia Mathematica and theory of descriptions directly address the conflict of Logicism with Intuitionism and Formalism (A.4.a). His contributions to mathematical logic and philosophy of language relate to developments in propositional and predicate logic (A.4.b) and Godel's incompleteness theorems (B.1.b).

The Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus and logical atomism relate to 20th-century logic (A.4) and developments in propositional logic (A.4.b). The later Philosophical Investigations on language games and meaning connects to problems in meaning and truth (B.2.a).

Godel's incompleteness theorems directly address metalogical studies and the properties of axiomatized systems (A.4.c), the conflict with Hilbert's Formalism (A.4.a), and the limitations of logic including Godel's incompleteness theorems (B.1.b).

Analytic philosophy's foundations in the work of Frege, Russell, Moore, and Wittgenstein, with emphasis on logical analysis and conceptual clarification, relate to 20th-century logic (A.4) and the nature and varieties of logic (B.1.a).
Coverage of the Nyaya school and Indian epistemological traditions connects to Indian logic (A.5.a), including origins in commentary on sutras and special types of inference.

Confucian philosophical tradition and the establishment of Neo-Confucianism in the 11th century relates to Chinese logic (A.5.b), particularly its origins in philosophical controversies and its neglect after Neo-Confucianism's establishment.

Mathematical proof, abstraction, and the philosophical underpinnings of mathematical truth connect to features and problems of logic (B.1.b) including questions about the relationship between logic and computability.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophy of meaning addressing word meaning, reference, truth, formal semantics, and speech act theory directly relates to problems in meaning and truth (B.2.a), including logical semantics of modal concepts.
Metaphysical analysis of objects, substance, existence, modality, and possible worlds directly addresses problems of ontology (B.2.b), including problems concerning individuation and existence.

Historical development of mathematics covering figures like Descartes, Euler, and Newton connects to the rise of mathematical logic during the Enlightenment (A.3.b) and the relationship between logic and mathematics in the 19th century (A.3.c).
